elements.sent-2411-006: Priors with non-smooth log densities have been widely used in Bayesian inverse problems, particularly in imaging, due to their sparsity inducing properties.
elements.sent-2411-007: To date, the majority of algorithms for handling such densities are based on proximal Langevin dynamics where one replaces the non-smooth part by a smooth approximation known as the Moreau envelope.
elements.sent-2411-008: In this work, we introduce a novel approach for sampling densities with $\ell_1$-priors based on a Hadamard product parameterization.
elements.sent-2411-009: This builds upon the idea that the Laplace prior has a Gaussian mixture representation and our method can be seen as a form of overparametrization: by increasing the number of variables, we construct a density from which one can directly recover the original density.
elements.sent-2411-010: This is fundamentally different from proximal-type approaches since our resolution is exact, while proximal-based methods introduce additional bias due to the Moreau-envelope smoothing.
elements.sent-2411-011: For our new density, we present its Langevin dynamics in continuous time and establish well-posedness and geometric ergodicity.
elements.sent-2411-012: We also present a discretization scheme for the continuous dynamics and prove convergence as the time-step diminishes.
elements.sent-2411-013: A common approach to Bayesian inverse problems of recovering $x$ given observations $y$ is to model the data distribution as $\rho(y\mid x) \propto \exp(-L(x,y))$ and the data prior as $\rho(x)\propto \exp(-R(x))$.
elements.sent-2411-014: The posterior distribution, representing the conditional distribution of $x$ given $y$, is then expressed as \begin{equation}\rho(x\mid y) = \frac{\rho(y\mid x)\rho(x) }{\rho(y)} \propto \exp(-R(x) - L(x,y)),\label{orig}\end{equation} where $\rho(y)$ is treated as a normalization constant and can often be discarded.
elements.sent-2411-015: A straightforward way to utilize this posterior distribution is to compute the maximum a posteriori (MAP) estimate $x_{\mathrm{MAP = \mathrm{arg\,max _{x \rho(x \mid y)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: This corresponds to solving an optimization problem \begin{equation}\label{eq:map} x_{\mathrm{MAP}} = \argmin_x R(x) + L(x,y).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-017: While the MAP approach yields a single point estimate of $x$, it does not provide a full statistical characterization of the inverse problem, such as uncertainty quantification.
elements.sent-2411-018: For more comprehensive analysis, one can instead sample from the posterior distribution $\rho(x \mid y)$, which enables the computation of statistical measures such as means, quantiles, and credible intervals, offering a deeper understanding of the solution and its associated uncertainties.
elements.sent-2411-019: In modern inverse problems, the prior is often modeled by a non-smooth log density (denoted by $R$ above).
elements.sent-2411-020: Non-smoothness plays a critical role in promoting structure within the solution.
elements.sent-2411-021: For instance, the $\ell_1$-norm $\norm{\cdot _1$ encourages sparsity, the nuclear norm promotes low-rank matrix solutions, and the total variation (TV) seminorm $\norm{D \,\cdot\, _1$ (composition of $\ell_1$ with gradient operator $D$) promotes sparsity in the gradient, leading to piecewise-smooth reconstructions.}}
elements.sent-2411-022: The choice of $R(x) = \norm{x _1$ has been pivotal in imaging inverse problems because it promotes sparse solutions (in some appropriate domain).}
elements.sent-2411-023: Sparsity has proven especially valuable in medical imaging, where high-resolution data is often undersampled to reduce scan time or radiation exposure.
elements.sent-2411-024: For example, sparse representations in wavelet domains have been key in compressed-sensing techniques, allowing for the reconstruction of images from far fewer measurements than traditionally required \cite{foucart2013invitation .}
elements.sent-2411-025: When the log density is a convex function, the corresponding optimization problem in \eqref{eq:map is convex, providing access to a wide array of efficient algorithms \cite{bach2012optimization .}}
elements.sent-2411-026: The $\ell_1$ setting has been particularly well studied and there is now a variety of efficient methods for handling non-smooth ($\ell_1$-like) convex objectives including proximal type methods and coordinate descent.
elements.sent-2411-027: Moreover, the scalability of these methods have made the MAP approach a canonical tool for large-scale imaging inverse problems, where computational efficiency and robustness are essential.
elements.sent-2411-028: On the other hand, as we discuss below, unlike the optimization setting where one can handle directly non-smooth priors, typical algorithms for the sampling the posterior distribution handle non-smooth priors via smoothed approximations (aka the Moreau envelope).

elements.subsection-2411-007: Links to the OU process and CIR process
elements.sent-2411-203: We highlight a link to some well-known SDEs, namely, the Cox--Ingersoll--Ross (CIR) process and Orstein--Uhlenbeck process.
elements.sent-2411-204: Girsanov's theorem allows us to transfer properties of these SDEs to our dynamics and therefore provide an alternative way of establishing well-posedness and strong Feller properties.
elements.sent-2411-205: This link will also serve as inspiration for the development of numerical schemes in \cref{sec:disc .}
elements.sent-2411-206: When the data term $G\equiv 0$, our Langevin dynamics decouple into some well-known SDEs for modelling interest rates.
elements.sent-2411-207: In particular, the Cox--Ingersoll--Ross (CIR) process is \begin{equation}\label{eq:cir1} dy_t = \kappa\,(\mu-y_t) \,dt + \theta \sqrt{y_t} \,dW_t\end{equation} This is known to have unique solutions with $ \mathbb{P (y_t>0, \forall t\geq 0) = 1$ if and only if the Feller test holds, which occurs for $2\kappa\mu\geq \theta^2$.}
elements.sent-2411-208: In this case, by defining $\hat u_t = \sqrt{y_t}$ (this is the so-called Lamperti transformation), Ito's formula implies that \begin{equation}\label{eq:cir_2} d\hat u_t = \frac{(4\kappa \mu-\theta^2)/8}{\hat u_t}\,dt -\frac{\kappa}{2}\,\hat u_t \,dt + \frac{\theta}{2} \,dW_t.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-209: This is precisely our $u$ process when $G\equiv 0$, with $\theta = 2\sqrt{2/\beta}$, $\kappa = 2\lambda$ and $\mu = 2/(\beta\lambda)$.
elements.sent-2411-210: Note that this correponds to the edge case of the Feller test as $2\kappa\mu = \theta^2$ \cite{feller1951two,carlo2001monte .}
elements.sent-2411-211: The Ornstein–Uhlenbeck process is given by \begin{equation}d\hat v_t =-\lambda\, \hat v_t\, dt + \sqrt{\frac{2}{\beta}}\,dW_t^2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-212: This is precisely our $v$ process with $G\equiv 0$.
elements.sent-2411-213: Given the connections mentioned above, the general idea for establishing well-posedness of our SDE is to interpret \eqref{main as `perturbed' CIR and OU processes.}
elements.sent-2411-214: Girsanov transformations will allow us to absorb the data term into the Brownian motion and hence inherit the required properties from CIR and OU processes.
elements.sent-2411-215: The results shown here can also be derived using the (simpler) arguments involving the cylindrical transformation \cref{cyl ; however, as a bonus, the arguments here additionally show that $u(t)$ is positive almost surely.}
